items.question: Greedy algorithms are guaranteed to be correct when:
options: the problem has the greedy-choice property AND optimal substructure
the algorithm uses a priority queue
items.answer: the problem has the greedy-choice property AND optimal substructure
items.explanation: Both conditions are needed; missing either makes greedy fallible.

items.question: Which of these is NOT solved optimally by a greedy algorithm in general?
items.explanation: Greedy by ratio fails on 0/1 knapsack; DP is required.

items.question: Why is Dijkstra called a greedy algorithm?
options: It commits to a node's distance the moment it is popped — the locally cheapest choice
It uses a priority queue (any algorithm with a PQ is greedy)
items.answer: It commits to a node's distance the moment it is popped — the locally cheapest choice

items.question: What is the time complexity of activity selection by end-time sort?
items.explanation: Dominant cost is the sort; the sweep is O(n).

items.question: Greedy decoding picks:
items.explanation: Sequence-level optimality requires search (beam) or sampling — argmax per step is just a local choice.
